title: AI-Assisted Code Refactoring
summary: How LLMs and agentic tools change the economics of refactoring at codebase scale — the prompt patterns, validation pipelines, review strategies, and risk management that make mass-refactor campaigns reliable.

chapters.title: The Mass-Refactor Problem
chapters.summary: Some refactorings are too large to do by hand — and the historic toolchain of regex, codemods, and AST passes hits a ceiling that LLMs can finally cross when handled with discipline.

chapters.title: LLMs as Refactoring Tools
chapters.summary: LLMs are a new category of refactoring tool — flexible enough to handle semantic transforms that AST-based tooling can't, but probabilistic in ways that demand new validation discipline.

chapters.title: Prompting for Refactoring
chapters.summary: Prompting is the leverage point of AI-assisted refactoring — the difference between a 95% success rate and a 50% success rate is almost always the prompt, not the model.

chapters.title: Pipelines for Mass Refactoring
chapters.summary: A mass refactoring is a pipeline — discovery, transform, validate, review, land — and the pipeline shape determines whether the campaign ships or stalls.

chapters.title: Tooling Landscape (2026)
chapters.summary: The 2026 tooling landscape for AI-assisted refactoring divides into IDE assistants, agentic CLIs, hybrid AST+LLM systems, and managed services — each with a distinct sweet spot and a distinct way of going wrong.

chapters.title: Validation: Tests, Types, and Behavior
chapters.summary: AI-generated refactorings need stronger validation than human-written ones — type checks, test runs, golden masters, semantic diffs, and runtime canaries are the toolkit that catches the silent breakage LLMs occasionally introduce.

chapters.title: Code Review at Scale
chapters.summary: When an AI campaign opens a thousand PRs, traditional file-by-file human review is impossible — the practice has to shift to sampling, automated heuristics, and choke-point review at the boundaries that matter.

chapters.title: Risk Management
chapters.summary: AI-driven mass refactoring multiplies blast radius by ~100× — and the practices that make it survivable are reversibility, gradual rollout, kill switches, and explicit staged-approval gates that keep the campaign recoverable when something goes wrong.

chapters.title: Case Studies and Patterns
chapters.summary: Five reference patterns — API migration, framework upgrade, dependency replacement, dead-code elimination, type-system migration — cover most real-world AI-assisted refactoring campaigns and let new ones be planned by analogy.

chapters.title: Strategy and Org Adoption
chapters.summary: AI-assisted refactoring is a capability you build into an organization, not a tool you install — and the patterns that make it stick are platform investment, success metrics, cost transparency, and clear "when not to" guardrails.
